title: CEA Economic Report of the President 2026 - Business Cycle Framing
content: The 2026 Economic Report of the President, prepared by the Council of Economic Advisers, grounds its review of U.S. economic activity in the National Bureau of Economic Research business-cycle chronology. Throughout Chapter 14, gray bars on every headline time-series chart indicate NBER-dated recessions, and cross-cycle comparisons are made against periods defined as outside recession by NBER. The CEA benchmarks the unemployment rate against its average outside recessions, compares participation rates and employment growth against pre-pandemic levels, and places current inflation, interest-rate, and labor-market prints within the reference frame of quarterly business-cycle peaks and troughs. In the supply-side decomposition of real output, the Report treats quarterly business-cycle peaks as the natural boundaries for comparing growth rates across expansions, using dates such as 1953:Q2, 1990:Q3, 2001:Q1, 2007:Q4, and 2019:Q4 as anchors. The Administration's forecast, summarized in the Report, also rests on this framing, assuming no recession over the 11-year projection window and discussing how immigration, productivity, and labor-force participation combine into a supply-side identity for real GDP growth. By leaning on the NBER chronology rather than a purely quantitative recession rule, the Report acknowledges that U.S. recessions are identified by a committee using multiple monthly indicators rather than a single trigger. The framework implies that a model predicting USREC should be evaluated against how closely its probability output tracks the NBER-dated peaks and troughs, and that narrative commentary on recession risk should be anchored in the same NBER reference the federal executive branch itself uses.
